[France](/wiki/France "France") [Landry Le May](/w/index.php?title=Landry_Le_May&action=edit&redlink=1 "Landry Le May (page does not exist)") | [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") [Georgii Pavlov](/w/index.php?title=Georgii_Pavlov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Georgii Pavlov (page does not exist)") | [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") [Arthur Wolfgang Mai](/w/index.php?title=Arthur_Wolfgang_Mai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arthur Wolfgang Mai (page does not exist)") | [[10]](#cite_note-BT2025-10)

is an annual [figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), organized and hosted by the Hungarian National Skating Federation ([Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Magyar Országos Korcsolyázó Szövetség*) at the Vasas Jégcentrum in [Budapest, Hungary](/wiki/Budapest,_Hungary "Budapest, Hungary"). The competition debuted in 2020 amidst the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Hungary "COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary"). The Budapest Trophy has been an [Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") event four times during its history as of 2025. Medals may be awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); and when the event is part of the Challenger Series, skaters earn [ISU World Standing](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking") points based on their results.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International figure skating competition

| Budapest Trophy | |
| --- | --- |
| [Logo of the Budapest Trophy](/wiki/File:Budapest_Trophy_logo.png) | |
| Status | Active |
| Genre | [International competition](/wiki/List_of_figure_skating_competitions "List of figure skating competitions") |
| Frequency | Annual |
| Venue | Vasas Jégcentrum |
| Location | [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") |
| Country | [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") Hungary |
| Inaugurated | 2020 |
| Previous event | 2025 |
| Organized by | Hungarian National Skating Federation |

The **Budapest Trophy** is an annual [figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), organized and hosted by the Hungarian National Skating Federation ([Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Magyar Országos Korcsolyázó Szövetség*) at the Vasas Jégcentrum in [Budapest, Hungary](/wiki/Budapest,_Hungary "Budapest, Hungary"). The competition debuted in 2020 amidst the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Hungary "COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary"). The Budapest Trophy has been an [Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") event four times during its history as of 2025. Medals may be awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); and when the event is part of the Challenger Series, skaters earn [ISU World Standing](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking") points based on their results.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budapest_Trophy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The [ISU Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") was introduced in 2014. It is a series of international figure skating competitions sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) and organized by [ISU member nations](/wiki/ISU_Member_Nations "ISU Member Nations"). The objective is to ensure consistent organization and structure within a series of international competitions linked together, providing opportunities for senior-level skaters to compete at the international level and also earn [ISU World Standing](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking") points.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Challenger Series events must be scheduled between 1 August and 15 December. When an event is held as part of the Challenger Series, it must host at least three of the four disciplines ([men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance")) and representatives from at least twelve different ISU member nations. The minimum number of entrants required for each discipline is: eight skaters each in men's singles and women's singles, five teams in pair skating, and six teams in ice dance.[[2]](#cite_note-ISU1894-2) While ISU member nations are limited to sending a maximum of three skaters or teams per discipline to each event, the Hungarian National Skating Federation can enter an unlimited number of entrants in their own event. Additionally, each skater or team is limited to participating in at most three Challenger Series events each season.[[3]](#cite_note-ISU1994-3)

The inaugural edition of the Budapest Trophy was intended to be the sixth event of the [2020–21 Challenger Series](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Challenger_Series "2020–21 ISU Challenger Series"), however all but two of the events were ultimately cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports#Figure_skating "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports"): the [2020 Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2020_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2020 CS Nebelhorn Trophy") and the [2020 Budapest Trophy](/wiki/2020_CS_Budapest_Trophy "2020 CS Budapest Trophy"). On 13 July, the ISU announced that the remaining Challenger Series events would be treated as separate individual competitions rather than part of a series; as a result, no Challenger Series ranking would be determined and no prize money distributed at the end of the series, although skaters could still earn Challenger Series points to apply toward their [world rankings](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking").[[4]](#cite_note-4) However, the ISU later revised their decision on 3 August, announcing that world ranking points would not be awarded due to the limited nature of the competitions.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On 1 October, the Hungarian National Skating Federation released a statement detailing the Hungarian government's [COVID-19 regulations](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Hungary "COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary") for competitors seeking to gain entry to Hungary.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The 2020 Budapest Trophy was held from 15 to 17 October at the Vasas Jégcentrum. Only 300 spectators were allowed into the arena each day, with temperatures taken prior to admittance and strict requirements for the wearing of masks.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Daniel Grassl](/wiki/Daniel_Grassl "Daniel Grassl") of Italy won the men's event, [Loena Hendrickx](/wiki/Loena_Hendrickx "Loena Hendrickx") of Belgium won the women's event, and [Oleksandra Nazarova](/wiki/Oleksandra_Nazarova "Oleksandra Nazarova") and [Maksym Nikitin](/wiki/Maksym_Nikitin "Maksym Nikitin") of Ukraine won the ice dance event.[[8]](#cite_note-BT2020-8)

The Budapest Trophy has been held every year since, although the 2021 and 2025 editions were not part of the Challenger Series.[[9]](#cite_note-BT2021-9)[[10]](#cite_note-BT2025-10)

## Senior medalists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Budapest_Trophy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Senior medalists")]

[![Ekaterina Kurakova at the 2024 World Championships](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Ekaterina_Kurakova_2024_Worlds_Free_Skate_2.jpg/250px-Ekaterina_Kurakova_2024_Worlds_Free_Skate_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ekaterina_Kurakova_2024_Worlds_Free_Skate_2.jpg)

[![Marjorie Lajoie and Zachary Lagha at the 2025 World Championships](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Marjorie_Lajoie_%26_Zachary_Lagha_2025_Worlds_Free_Dance_09.jpg/250px-Marjorie_Lajoie_%26_Zachary_Lagha_2025_Worlds_Free_Dance_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marjorie_Lajoie_%26_Zachary_Lagha_2025_Worlds_Free_Dance_09.jpg)

The 2025 Budapest Trophy champions: [Ekaterina Kurakova](/wiki/Ekaterina_Kurakova "Ekaterina Kurakova") of Poland ([women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating")); and [Marjorie Lajoie](/wiki/Marjorie_Lajoie "Marjorie Lajoie") and [Zachary Lagha](/wiki/Zachary_Lagha "Zachary Lagha") of Canada ([ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"))  
*Not pictured: [Landry Le May](/w/index.php?title=Landry_Le_May&action=edit&redlink=1 "Landry Le May (page does not exist)") of France ([men's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"))*

**CS**: [Challenger Series event](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series")
